dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for chest pain.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Uh, chest pain on the left, shortness of breath, lasting over two days. It's an 8/10.
You are alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation (A&O x4), with a GCS of 15.
Your airway is patent and unobstructed with no immediate life threats noted.
Your breathing appears unlabored, regular, but slightly elevated in rate.
No signs of active bleeding. Strong, equal radial pulses with warm, dry skin.
Clear and equal breath sounds are present at the base and apex bilaterally.
Initial vitals are blood pressure 116/77, pulse 64, respiratory rate 22, and oxygen saturation 99% on room air.
Glucose level is 80-this is within normal limits.
Tenderness was noted on the left side of your chest. Does the pain worsen with deep breaths or coughing?
You describe chest pain occurring at night, localized, and made worse by deep breathing or coughing. It's been persistent for two days.
I will now start an IV in your left antecubital vein as per standing order protocol.
No nuchal rigidity found during neck assessment.
No abdominal tenderness, rigidity, or guarding. No deformity, abrasions, or bruises noted on abdominal or chest surfaces.
You have bee venom allergy, no known drug allergies.
The glucose meter reads 0, but this is likely due to a sensor error since earlier readings were 80.
You have no reported changes in gastrointestinal or genitourinary habits.
No nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea. No fever or chills.
Current vitals are 112/73 blood pressure, pulse 64, respiratory rate 19, and 95% oxygen saturation.
Normal strength and coordination noted in all extremities with no deformity or swelling.
You remain alert, oriented, and cooperative with clear communication and no signs of distress.
You are currently taking rivaroxaban, risperidone, and Prozac as prescribed.
There is mild increased effort in breathing but no accessory muscle use.
Respiratory rate is now at 16-still unlabored with equal breath sounds.
Extraocular movements are normal, no visual disturbances reported.
Latest vitals show blood pressure at 105/69, pulse 59, and oxygen saturation at 97%.
The 12-lead EKG shows normal sinus rhythm for your age and no signs of acute changes.
EKG rhythm is normal and consistent with a 40-year-old male.
Breath sounds are clear and equal bilaterally.
Your capillary nail bed refill is less than two seconds with a warm and dry color.
Pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light (PERRL), approximately 3 mm in diameter.
No deformity, contusion, abrasion, puncture, burns, or lacerations noted on torso or extremities.
You'll be transported in semi-Fowler's position to assist with breathing and comfort.
Vitals have remained stable, with no acute deterioration observed.
Let me reassess your vital signs to confirm stability before transport.
Pain is still 8 out of 10. Has this changed in the last few hours?
Your breathing is still 16 breaths per minute and unlabored.
You remain GCS 15 with no decline observed. Communication is clear and appropriate.
You have not started or stopped any new medications since last dose.
Oxygen saturation remains 97%, stable and acceptable.
Transport will proceed in a semi-Fowler's position with appropriate restraints and monitoring.
The hospital has been notified with all critical findings, including your vitals and 12-lead EKG.
Vitals are steady and you continue to have no signs of distress.
VCU Health Systems is the recommended destination for continued monitoring and evaluation.
A concise pre-arrival report has been sent to the hospital with your current status and interventions.
We are now ready to book a transfer. All equipment and data are accounted for.
Patient is securely on stretcher with shoulder and lap belts. Position confirmed as semi-Fowler.
Please note: 40-year-old male for billing documentation and protocol adherence.
